questions.text: The process by which various components of cells including its organelle can be isolated is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Homogenization is the process of breaking down cells into their component parts. It involves grinding cells in a suitable medium with the correct pH, ionic composition, temperature, and certain enzymes. This can be done using physical methods, such as blending, or chemical methods, such as using detergents.

Cell fractionation is a multi-step process that typically involves homogenization, followed by centrifugation to separate the cell components by their density. The different cell components can then be further purified using additional methods, such as gel electrophoresis or ultracentrifugation.

Cell fixation is the process of preserving cells so that they can be examined under a microscope. This is typically done by treating cells with a chemical fixative, such as formaldehyde or glutaraldehyde.
Gel electrophoresis is a technique used to separate molecules based on their size and charge. This can be used to separate DNA, RNA, or proteins.
Ultracentrifugation is a technique that uses high centrifugal force to separate molecules based on their density. This can be used to isolate organelles, such as mitochondria or nuclei.

questions.explanation: Option A: Homogenization is a process used in cell fractionation where cells are broken open to release their contents. It involves methods like mechanical disruption, sonication, or enzymatic digestion. Homogenization helps in breaking down the cell structure to release cellular components for further separation and analysis. Option B: Cell fractionation describes the process which is used to to separate individual cell components while maintaining the individual functions of each component. This enables each of the components or organelles to be isolated separately and examined. It involves homogenization first so that the organelles are released from the cells, and the organelles are separated via centrifugation. Hence, this term covers the entire process involved in isolating organelles, so it is the correct answer.Option C: Cell fixation is the preservation of cells or tissues using chemical agents. It involves treating cells with fixatives to immobilize their structures and preserve their components. Fixation helps maintain cellular morphology and prevents degradation or changes in cellular components during subsequent processing steps. Option D: Gel electrophoresis is a technique used to separate and analyze biomolecules, such as proteins or nucleic acids, based on their size and charge. The molecules are placed in a gel matrix and subjected to an electric field. They migrate through the gel at different rates based on their size and charge, allowing for their separation.Option E: Ultracentrifugation is a high-speed centrifugation method used to separate particles based on their density or size. Samples are spun at very high speeds using ultracentrifuges, generating high centrifugal forces. This causes particles to sediment based on their density or size gradients. Ultracentrifugation is commonly used in cell fractionation to separate cellular components, like organelles, based on their density. This is a step involved in the whole cell fractionation process, so option B is the preferred answer over this.

Cutin is a type of fatty substance that is found in the outer layer of plant cell walls, but it is not found in fungal cell walls. Fungal cell walls are made up of chitin, which is not a fatty substance.

Option C: Suberin: A type of waxy substance found in the cell walls of plants. Option D: Cutin: A type of fatty substance found in the outer layer of plant cell walls. Option E: Proteins are found in all cell walls, and they play a variety of roles, such as providing structure, anchoring the cell wall to other structures, and interacting with the environment.

questions.text: DNA synthesis takes place in _ phase of the cell cycle.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is a resting phase of the cell cycle. Cells in G0 are not dividing, but they are still metabolically active. Some cells, such as nerve cells and muscle cells, may remain in G0 for the rest of their lives.

This is the first growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell grows in size and synthesizes proteins and RNA. The cell also checks its DNA for damage and repairs any damage that is found. If the DNA is damaged beyond repair, the cell may enter a programmed cell death (apoptosis) pathway.

This is the second growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell prepares to divide by synthesizing proteins and RNA. The cell also checks its DNA for damage one last time before dividing.
This is the synthesis phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell replicates its DNA. The DNA is replicated twice, once for each of the two new cells that will be produced.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct . During the S (Synthesis) phase of the cell cycle, the cell replicates its entire DNA content, creating two identical sister chromatids for each chromosome. This process begins with DNA helicase unwinding the double helix, after which DNA polymerase uses each original strand as a template to build a new, complementary strand, following base-pairing rules (A with T, G with C). The result is two identical DNA molecules, ensuring that each daughter cell receives a complete and accurate set of genetic material after cell division. G0 phase: This phase is a resting phase of the cell cycle. Cells in G0 are not dividing, but they are still metabolically active. G1 phase: This is the first growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell grows in size and synthesizes proteins and RNA. G2 phase: This is the second growth phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell prepares to divide by synthesizing proteins and RNA.S phase: This is the synthesis phase of the cell cycle. During this phase, the cell replicates its DNA.

questions.text: The RNA found in Ribosomes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: mRNA (messenger RNA) is a type of RNA that carries the genetic information from DNA to the ribosomes. It is transcribed from DNA in the nucleus and then exported to the cytoplasm, where it binds to ribosomes. The ribosomes then use the mRNA to synthesize proteins.

Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is the most abundant type of RNA in cells. It is found in ribosomes, which are the cellular structures that are responsible for protein synthesis. rRNA makes up about 80% of the mass of ribosomes. They are made up of two subunits, a large subunit and a small subunit. Both subunits contain rRNA, as well as a number of proteins. It is involved in the assembly of ribosomes and in the translation of mRNA into proteins.

tRNA (transfer RNA) is a type of RNA that carries amino acids to ribosomes. It is transcribed from DNA in the nucleus and then exported to the cytoplasm. Each tRNA molecule is specific for one amino acid. When a ribosome is translating an mRNA molecule, it will bind to a tRNA molecule that has the corresponding amino acid. The amino acid is then added to the growing polypeptide chain.
Polysome is a cluster of ribosomes that are translating the same mRNA molecule. Polysomes are often found in cells that are actively synthesizing proteins.

A gene is a segment of DNA that contains the instructions for making a specific protein. Proteins are the building blocks of cells and tissues, and they play a role in almost every function of the body. Genes are located on chromosomes, which are thread-like structures in the nucleus of cells. Each chromosome contains thousands of genes.

It is involved in the assembly of ribosomes and in the translation of mRNA into proteins.

questions.text: The outermost boundary in most of the leaf cell is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outermost boundary in most leaf cells is the cell wall. It is a rigid layer that surrounds the cell membrane and provides support and protection for the cell. It is made up of cellulose.
The cell membrane is a thin, flexible layer that surrounds all cells.
The tonoplast is the membrane that surrounds the vacuole, which is a large organelle found in plant cells.

A unit membrane is a single layer of phospholipids that forms the basic structure of all cell membranes. It is made up of the same components as the cell membrane, but it does not form a complete barrier around the cell. Instead, it forms a series of interconnected sheets that allow for the passage of substances between different parts of the cell.

Polar substances are molecules that have a positive and negative end. They are not boundaries of cells, but they can interact with the cell membrane. Polar substances can dissolve in water, and they can also pass through the cell membrane by diffusion.

questions.explanation: Option A: The outermost boundary of most leaf cells is the cell wall. It is a rigid layer that surrounds the cell membrane and provides support and protection for the cell. It is made up of cellulose, a long-chain carbohydrate. The cell wall is not present in animal cells. Option B: The cell membrane is the outermost boundary of all cells, but it is not the outermost boundary of most leaf cells. Option C: The tonoplast is the membrane that surrounds the vacuole, which is a large organelle found in plant cells. It is not the outermost boundary of the cell. Option D: A unit membrane is a single layer of phospholipids that forms the basic structure of all cell membranes. It is not the outermost boundary of any cell. questions.text: In the human cell, _ is responsible for oxidizing hydrogen peroxide.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysosomes are another type of cell organelle that contains enzymes that can break down waste products and foreign invaders. However, lysosomes do not produce hydrogen peroxide.
Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell, responsible for producing ATP, the cell's energy currency. They do not produce hydrogen peroxide.

Peroxisomes are small, membrane-bound organelles that are found in most eukaryotic cells. They contain enzymes that carry out oxidation reactions, which can produce hydrogen peroxide as a byproduct. Hydrogen peroxide is a reactive molecule that can damage cells, but peroxisomes also contain enzymes that can break down hydrogen peroxide, preventing it from causing harm.

Glyoxysomes are a type of peroxisome that is found in plant cells. They are involved in the breakdown of fatty acids to produce carbohydrates. questions.text: The soluble part of the blood is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Karyolymph is the fluid portion of the nucleus of a cell. It is made up of water, salts, and dissolved proteins. Karyolymph does not contain any organelles, such as chromosomes or ribosomes.
Nucleoplasm is a gel-like substance within the nucleus containing chromatin. Involved in nuclear processes but not associated with the soluble part of the blood.

Protoplasm is the living material that makes up cells. It is made up of water, proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, and nucleic acids. Protoplasm is not soluble in blood because it is a complex mixture of molecules that are held together by bonds.

The soluble part of the blood is called serum. Serum is the liquid portion of blood that remains after blood clotting and the removal of blood cells, fibrinogen, and clotting factors during the process of coagulation.

questions.explanation: The soluble part of the blood is called serum. Serum is the liquid portion of blood that remains after blood clotting and the removal of blood cells, fibrinogen, and clotting factors during the process of coagulation. It contains various proteins, electrolytes, hormones, antibodies, and other substances that are dissolved in the plasma. Serum is often used in medical testing to analyze different components present in the blood, as it provides valuable information about a person's health and physiological status.

questions.text: The animals that feed on organic debris from decomposing plants and animals are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Herbivores are animals that primarily consume plants or plant-based material. They obtain their nutrients by feeding on vegetation, such as leaves, stems, roots, or fruits. Herbivores play a crucial role in the food chain as primary consumers, converting plant matter into energy and serving as a food source for other animals. Examples of herbivores include cows, rabbits, deer, and caterpillars.

Carnivores are animals that primarily feed on other animals. They obtain their nutrients by consuming the flesh or body parts of other animals. Carnivores are usually higher up in the food chain and are often characterised by sharp teeth and strong jaws adapted for hunting and capturing prey. Examples of carnivores include lions, tigers, wolves, and sharks.

Omnivores are organisms that feed on both plants and animals. They have the ability to consume and digest a variety of food sources. Omnivores can eat both plant material, such as fruits, vegetables, and seeds, as well as animal material, such as meat, insects, and fish. Humans are an example of omnivores, as they consume both plant-based and animal-based foods. Other examples of omnivores include bears, raccoons, and pigs.

The animals that feed on organic debris from decomposing plants and animals are called detritivores. Detritivores are a type of heterotrophic organism that obtains nutrients by consuming dead organic matter, such as decaying plant material, dead animals, and feces. They play a crucial role in ecosystems by recycling nutrients and breaking down organic material, which helps to decompose and recycle nutrients back into the environment. Examples of detritivores include earthworms, dung beetles, vultures, and certain species of insects and crustaceans.

questions.explanation: Option A: Herbivores are organisms that feed on plants. They include cows, rabbits, and deer. Herbivores are important consumers because they help to control the growth of plant populations. Option B: Carnivores are organisms that feed on animals. They include lions, wolves, and sharks. Carnivores are important consumers because they help to control the populations of herbivores. Option C: Omnivores are organisms that feed on both plants and animals. They include humans, bears, and pigs. Omnivores are important consumers because they can adapt to different food sources. Option D: Detritivores are organisms that feed on dead or decaying organic matter. They include earthworms, termites, and fungi. Detritivores play an important role in the ecosystem by breaking down organic matter and recycling nutrients.

questions.text: Actinia is the biological name of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The biological name of sea anemone is Actinia. They are a type of cnidarian, which are animals that have stinging cells. Sea anemones are found in shallow marine waters all over the world. They attach themselves to rocks or other hard surfaces and use their tentacles to capture prey.

Corals are also cnidarians, but they are more closely related to jellyfish. Corals are colonial animals, which means that they form large colonies of individual polyps. The polyps are connected by a common tissue and share a digestive system. Corals are found in warm, shallow waters and build reefs.

Obelia is a type of hydrozoan, which is another type of cnidarian. Hydrozoans are similar to sea anemones, but they are typically smaller and have a more delicate body structure. Obelia is found in freshwater and marine habitats.

Jellyfish are also cnidarians, but they are more closely related to box jellyfish than to sea anemones. Jellyfish are free-swimming animals that have a bell-shaped body and trailing tentacles. They are found in all oceans of the world.
Frogs are not cnidarians. They are amphibians, which means that they spend part of their life cycle in water and part on land. Frogs are found on all continents except Antarctica.

questions.text: The simplest form in Kingdom Animalia belongs to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Eumetazoa is a major subdivision within the animal kingdom that includes most animals. These organisms are characterized by having true tissues, which are specialized groups of cells that perform specific functions. Eumetazoans have well-defined body plans and exhibit different levels of complexity, ranging from simple organisms like jellyfish to highly complex animals like mammals

Bilateria is a branch of animals that are bilaterally symmetrical, meaning they can be divided into two equal halves along a sagittal plane. This group includes the vast majority of animal species. Bilaterians have three primary germ layers during embryonic development: the ectoderm (outer layer), mesoderm (middle layer), and endoderm (inner layer). These layers give rise to different tissues and organs, allowing for greater complexity in body structure and function.

Protozoa are a diverse group of unicellular eukaryotic organisms that belong to the Kingdom Protista. While they are often studied as part of the broader field of microbiology, they are not classified as animals. Protozoa exhibit a wide range of characteristics and can be found in various environments, including freshwater, marine habitats, and soil. They have complex internal structures, such as organelles for movement (like cilia or flagella) and feeding (like specialized vacuoles). Protozoa play essential roles in ecosystems as predators, grazers, and decomposers.

The simplest form in Kingdom Animalia belongs to the group known as Parazoa. They are the simplest animals in the kingdom Animalia. Parazoans are multicellular animals that lack true tissues and organs. They include the phylum Porifera, which consists of sponges. Sponges have a porous body structure with specialized cells that perform various functions. Parazoans are considered the most primitive animals within the animal kingdom and have a simpler body organization compared to other animal groups like Eumetazoa, Bilateria, and Protostomia. Parazoans are sessile or sedentary organisms that rely on filter feeding to obtain nutrients.

Protostomia is a major subdivision of bilaterally symmetrical animals that includes various phyla such as Arthropoda, Mollusca, and Annelida. Protostomes undergo protostomic development during embryogenesis, where the blastopore (the first opening formed during gastrulation) becomes the mouth. They exhibit a wide range of body plans and complexity, including segmented bodies, jointed appendages, and specialized feeding structures. Protostomes have diverse ecological roles and are found in various habitats, both terrestrial and aquatic.

Most animals in the kingdom Animalia are bilaterians. Protozoa is a group of single-celled organisms that are not animals. questions.text: The Porifera are pore - bearing animals, commonly called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Corals are marine invertebrate animals belonging to the phylum Cnidaria and made up of colonies of polyps. Polyps are small, cylindrical animals that have a mouth surrounded by tentacles. Corals build calcium carbonate skeletons that form coral reefs, which provide habitat for a diverse range of marine life. They reproduce both sexually and asexually.

Porifera is the phylum of animals commonly known as sponges. They are called "pore-bearing animals" because they have numerous tiny openings called pores on their body surface. These pores allow water to enter the sponge, facilitating filter feeding and gas exchange. They live in marine and freshwater environments. Sponges are multicellular organisms belonging to the Parazoa group, and they are distinct from corals, hydras, and anemones, which belong to other phyla within the animal kingdom.

Hydras are small, freshwater animals belonging to the phylum Cnidaria. They are solitary polyps that exhibit radial symmetry. Hydras have a tubular body with tentacles surrounding their mouth, which they use to capture prey. They have specialized stinging cells called cnidocytes that contain nematocysts, which can immobilize or kill their prey. Hydras can reproduce both sexually and asexually through budding.

Anemones are marine animals that also belong to the phylum Cnidaria. They are closely related to corals and jellyfish. Anemones are solitary polyps that have a cylindrical body with a central mouth surrounded by tentacles. They typically attach to rocks or other substrates and are capable of retracting their tentacles when disturbed. Anemones use their stinging cells to capture and immobilize prey, which they then consume.

questions.text: Study of environmental relations is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Physiology is the study of the functions of living organisms. It includes the study of how organisms' bodies work, how they respond to their environment, and how they reproduce.
Pulmonology is the study of the lungs and the respiratory system. It includes the study of how the lungs work, how they exchange gases with the blood, and how they are affected by disease.
Morphology is the study of the form and structure of living organisms. It includes the study of how organisms' bodies are shaped, how their organs are arranged, and how their tissues are organized.

The study of environmental relations is called ecology. Ecology is a branch of biology that studies the interactions between living organisms and their environment. It includes the study of how organisms interact with each other, with the physical environment, and with the other living things in their environment.

The order cell > tissue > organ > system is the correct order because it follows the hierarchy of biological organization, from the smallest unit (cells) to the largest unit (organ systems). Tissues are groups of specialized cells that have similar structures and functions. Cells with similar characteristics come together to form tissues, such as muscle tissue, nerve tissue, or connective tissue. Organs are composed of different types of tissues working together to perform specific functions. Examples of organs include the heart, lungs, brain, and liver. Examples of systems include the circulatory system, nervous system, and digestive system. Systems integrate the functions of multiple organs to achieve specific physiological tasks.

questions.text: Blood containing CO2 is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxygenated blood is bright red because of the presence of oxygen. Oxygen binds to hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells, and gives the blood its red color.

Deoxygenated blood is darker red or even blue because it has less oxygen. When oxygen leaves the blood, carbon dioxide binds to hemoglobin instead. Carbon dioxide makes the blood appear darker because it absorbs more light.

Blood containing CO2 is reddish purple in color. This is because CO2 binds to hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells, and changes its shape. The change in shape makes the blood appear darker and more purple. Blood that is high in oxygen is bright red. As blood travels through the body and delivers oxygen to the tissues, it picks up CO2 as a waste product. The more CO2 that is in the blood, the darker it will appear.

Blood containing CO2 can also appear reddish blue. This is because the amount of carbon dioxide in the blood can vary depending on factors such as exercise and breathing. When the body is working harder, it produces more carbon dioxide, which makes the blood appear darker.

questions.explanation: Blood containing carbon dioxide (CO2) is reddish purple in color. This is because CO2 binds to hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells, and changes its shape. The change in shape makes the blood appear darker and more purple. Blood that is high in oxygen is bright red. As blood travels through the body and delivers oxygen to the tissues, it picks up CO2 as a waste product. The more CO2 that is in the blood, the darker it will appear. Overall, blood containing CO2 is reddish purple in color. The darker the blood, the more CO2 it contains.

questions.text: The mechanism by which substances are removed from blood and are directly added to the tubular fluids is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The kidney filter blood in 3 ways. First, the nephrons filter blood that runs through the capillary network in the glomerulus. Almost all solutes, except for proteins, are filtered out into the glomerulus by a process called glomerular filtration.
Excretion is a process in which metabolic waste is eliminated from an organism. In vertebrates, this is primarily carried out by the lungs, kidneys, and skin.
Tubular secretion is the transfer of materials from peritubular capillaries to the renal tubular lumen; it is the opposite process of reabsorption.
The filtrate is collected in renal tubules. Most of the solutes get reabsorbed in the proximal convoluted tubule by a process called tubular reabsorption.

questions.explanation: Tubular secretion is the process where substances are actively transported from the blood into the renal tubular fluid for excretion in urine. This is distinct from glomerular filtration (where substances are filtered into the tubule passively) and tubular reabsorption (where substances are moved from the tubule back to the blood). Excretion is the overall removal of waste from the body.

options.explanation: TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) from the hypothalamus.
Oxytocin is released directly by the hypothalamus into the bloodstream. It is involved in childbirth, breastfeeding, and social bonding.

ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) from the hypothalamus. ACTH stimulates the release of cortisol from the adrenal glands.

FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) and LH (luteinizing hormone) are released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to releasing factors called follicle-stimulating hormone-releasing hormone (FSHRH) and luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH) from the hypothalamus. FSH and LH are involved in the regulation of reproduction.

GH (growth hormone) is released by the anterior pituitary gland in response to a releasing factor called growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) from the hypothalamus. GHRH stimulates the release of GH, which is involved in growth and development.

Primary active transport is a process that moves molecules against their concentration gradient across a cell membrane by using metabolic energy, primarily from ATP hydrolysis. Glucose transport is a quick process therefore not requiring any expenditure of energy making the option incorrect.

questions.text: Which artery supplies blood to the liver?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs.

The hepatic artery supplies blood to the liver. It is a branch of the celiac trunk, which is a large artery that arises from the abdominal aorta. The hepatic artery carries oxygenated blood to the liver, where it is used to metabolize nutrients and toxins.
The celiac artery supplies blood to the stomach, pancreas, and duodenum.
The thoracic artery supplies blood to the chest wall, lungs, and diaphragm.

questions.explanation: The hepatic artery delivers oxygen-rich blood from the celiac trunk to the liver for metabolism and detoxification.

questions.text: Movement of the hip joint is which type of synovial joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gliding joints permit limited movement in two directions and are typically found in the wrists and ankles. They do not provide the extensive range of motion seen in the hip joint.

The hip joint is classified as a ball-and-socket joint, which allows for multi-directional movement and rotation. This joint type enables a wide range of motions, including flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and both internal and external rotation.
Pivot joints allow for rotational movement around a single axis, such as the joint between the first and second vertebrae in the neck. They do not accommodate the complex movements of the hip joint.

Hinge joints permit movement primarily in one direction, similar to a door. This type of joint is exemplified by the elbow and knee, which contrasts with the multi-directional capabilities of the hip joint.

questions.explanation: The hip joint allows movement in all directions, including rotation. This wide range of motion is characteristic of a ball and socket synovial joint.

questions.text: A hormone called _ controls the secretion of gastric juice.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hormone that controls the secretion of gastric juice is called gastrin. Gastrin is produced by special cells in the stomach called G cells. It plays a key role in stimulating the release of gastric acid, which is important for digestion and the breakdown of food in the stomach. Gastrin is released in response to various stimuli, such as the presence of food in the stomach or the stretching of the stomach wall.

Secretin is a hormone that controls the secretion of pancreatic juice. It is produced by S cells in the duodenum. Secretin stimulates the secretion of bicarbonate ions by pancreatic duct cells. Bicarbonate ions neutralize the acidic contents of the stomach, which helps to protect the small intestine from damage.
Thyroxine is a hormones that control metabolism. It is produced by the thyroid gland.
Iodothyronine is a hormones that control metabolism. It is produced by the thyroid gland.
Parathormone is a hormone produced by the parathyroid glands. It controls calcium levels in the blood. It does not have a direct effect on the secretion of gastric juice.

questions.text: Process of uncontrolled cell division is due to which one of the following reasons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: DNA replication is the process by which DNA is copied in preparation for cell division. Mutations can occur during DNA replication if there is an error in the copying process. These errors can be caused by environmental factors, such as exposure to radiation or chemicals, or they can be inherited from parents.

The process of uncontrolled cell division is due to mutations in the DNA. Mutations are changes in the DNA sequence that can occur during DNA replication. These changes can lead to the production of proteins that malfunction, which can disrupt the cell cycle and cause cells to divide uncontrollably. This is because mutations can occur in any cell, and they can be passed on to daughter cells. As a result, mutations can accumulate over time and lead to the development of cancer.

Translation is the process by which RNA is used to produce proteins. If there is an error in translation, it can lead to the production of a protein that malfunctions. This can also disrupt the cell cycle and cause cells to divide uncontrollably.

Mutations can also occur during transcription, which is the process of copying a segment of DNA into RNA. RNA is then used to produce proteins. If there is an error in transcription, it can lead to the production of a protein that malfunctions. This can also disrupt the cell cycle and cause cells to divide uncontrollably.

questions.text: Which of the following is derived from the Latin word venom which means poisonous?

options.explanation: Bacteria are single-celled organisms that can cause disease. They are not derived from the Latin word venemus. The word bacteria comes from the Greek word bacterium, which means "small staff".
Fungi are multicellular organisms that can cause disease. They are not derived from the Latin word venemus. The word fungi comes from the Latin word fungus, which means "mushroom".

The Latin word virus also comes from venume, and it refers to a small, infectious agent that can cause disease. The word virus was first used in English in the 16th century. questions.text: Functions of the brainstem include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brainstem plays a crucial role in integrating and coordinating reflexes that help maintain an upright posture and balance in response to changes in body position.
The brainstem contains respiratory centers that regulate and control the automatic process of breathing, including the rhythm and depth of respiration.

The brainstem, along with other brain structures, contributes to the maintenance of balance, coordination, and posture. It receives sensory information from the inner ear and coordinates motor responses to help keep the body stable and oriented.

The initiation of voluntary movements primarily involves higher brain regions, such as the motor cortex. While the brainstem is involved in coordinating and refining movements, it does not directly initiate voluntary movements.

The brainstem includes structures responsible for controlling eye movements and coordinating the fixation of gaze. It ensures that the eyes can move together smoothly and accurately to focus on specific targets.

questions.text: Cardiac output is increased by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hypoxia, which is a low oxygen level in the tissues, can actually increase cardiac output. The body compensates for reduced oxygen availability by increasing heart rate and stroke volume to deliver more oxygenated blood to the tissues.
During exercise, cardiac output increases to meet the increased demand for oxygen and nutrients by the working muscles. This is achieved by an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.
During sleep, cardiac output typically decreases compared to wakefulness. The body's metabolic demands are generally lower during sleep, resulting in a decreased need for an elevated cardiac output.

Pregnancy leads to an increase in cardiac output to meet the increased demands of the growing fetus and maternal tissues. Hormonal and physiological changes during pregnancy result in an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.

questions.explanation: During sleep, cardiac output decreases due to lower metabolic demand, whereas exercise, hypoxia, and pregnancy increase cardiac output.

questions.text: Sequence of stop codon in RNA is:

options.explanation: TAG is a start codon, which means that it signals the beginning of protein synthesis. It codes for the amino acid methionine.
AUG is not a stop codon; it is the start codon in the genetic code. AUG codes for the amino acid "Methionine" and also serves as the initiation signal for protein synthesis.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs represents an analogous feature?

questions.text: The position of an organism in a food chain of an ecosystem called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The level of the ecosystem is not a term used in ecology.
A food chain is a linear sequence of organisms through which nutrients and energy pass as one organism eats another. However, it does not represent the position of an organism in the food chain.

A food web is a more complex model of feeding relationships in an ecosystem, where multiple food chains are interconnected. However, it does not represent the position of an organism in the food chain.

The correct answer is trophic level. A trophic level is a step in a food chain, and it represents the organism's position in the food chain relative to its source of energy. The first trophic level is made up of producers, which are organisms that can make their own food. The second trophic level is made up of consumers, which are organisms that eat producers. The third trophic level is made up of consumers that eat other consumers, and so on.
The energy pyramid is a graphical representation of the amount of energy available at each trophic level in a food chain. However, it does not represent the position of an organism in the food chain.

questions.text: Which of the following trophic levels has the largest biomass in ecosystems?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Decomposers, such as bacteria and fungi, break down dead organic matter and waste materials, returning nutrients to the ecosystem. While they play a crucial role in nutrient recycling, their biomass is typically lower compared to other trophic levels since they rely on the energy stored in organic matter rather than producing their own energy.

Primary consumers, also known as herbivores, are organisms that consume producers (plants) as their main source of food. Examples include animals like rabbits, deer, and grasshoppers. While primary consumers can have significant biomass, it is generally lower than that of producers since energy is lost as it moves through the food chain.

Secondary consumers are organisms that feed on primary consumers. They are typically carnivores or omnivores. Examples include predators like lions, wolves, and hawks. The biomass of secondary consumers is even lower than that of primary consumers since energy is further lost in each trophic level.

Producers typically have the largest biomass in ecosystems. Producers, also known as autotrophs, are organisms that can convert energy from sunlight or inorganic compounds into organic matter through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis. They include plants, algae, and some bacteria. Since they can produce their own food, they form the base of the food chain and support the energy needs of other organisms in the ecosystem. As a result, the biomass of producers tends to be higher than that of other trophic levels.

Herbivores are primary consumers that specifically feed on plants. They rely on producers for their energy and biomass. While herbivores can have substantial populations and biomass, it is typically lower than that of producers since energy is lost during energy transfer from one trophic level to the next.

questions.text: Germ Theory of disease was proposed by:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.topic: Biology and its Major Fields of Specialization

options.explanation: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, a Dutch microscopist, was the first person to observe microorganisms in the 17th century.
Louis Pasteur, a French chemist and microbiologist, conducted experiments that showed that microorganisms can cause fermentation and spoilage. He also developed vaccines for rabies and anthrax.
Walther Flemming, a Danish biologist, discovered chromosomes and showed that they are made up of DNA.

The germ theory of disease was proposed by Robert Koch. He is a German physician and microbiologist who is considered the "father of bacteriology". In the late 19th century, Koch developed four criteria, now known as Koch's postulates, to establish that a specific microorganism causes a specific disease. These criteria are still used today to confirm the cause of a disease. The germ theory of disease has revolutionized the way we understand and treat disease. It has led to the development of vaccines, antibiotics, and other treatments that have saved millions of live.
Edward Jenner, an English physician, developed the first vaccine against smallpox.

questions.text: Optimum temperature for enzymes of human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: For humans, enzymes function best at normal body temperature, which is about 37 °C (98.6 °F). At temperatures much higher than this (above ~40–45 °C), enzymes begin to denature, losing their three-dimensional shape and thus their catalytic function.

questions.text: Capillaries present around the loop of Henle:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vasa recta are a series of straight capillaries that descend from the cortical efferent arterioles and run parallel to the loop of Henle. They are important for maintaining the osmolarity of the medulla, which is essential for the kidneys to concentrate urine. The vasa recta are able to do this because they are permeable to water but not to solutes. This allows them to absorb water from the medullary interstitium, which helps to keep the medulla hypertonic (high in solute concentration).

Peritubular capillaries are a network of capillaries that surround the entire nephron. They are responsible for reabsorbing water and solutes from the filtrate. Glomeruli are the tufts of capillaries that filter blood in the kidneys. They are not located around the loop of Henle. Glomeruli are responsible for filtering about 20% of the blood that flows through the kidneys. The filtrate that is produced by the glomeruli is then passed through the rest of the nephron, where water and solutes are reabsorbed and urine is formed.

questions.text: The only possible treatment in end stage renal failure is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hemodialysis is a form of renal replacement therapy that helps filter waste products and excess fluid from the blood in individuals with kidney failure. While it is a treatment option for end-stage renal failure, it is not the only possible treatment. Kidney transplantation is generally considered the most effective long-term treatment option.

Peritoneal dialysis is another form of renal replacement therapy that uses the lining of the abdomen (peritoneum) as a filter to remove waste products and excess fluid from the body. Like hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis is a viable treatment option for end-stage renal failure, but it is not the only possible treatment. Kidney transplantation remains the preferred long-term solution.

Lithotripsy is a procedure used to break up kidney stones or other urinary tract stones using shock waves. While it can be a helpful treatment for stone-related conditions, it is not a suitable treatment for end-stage renal failure. End-stage renal failure refers to the severe and permanent loss of kidney function, which requires more comprehensive interventions such as dialysis or transplantation.

The most effective and definitive treatment for end-stage renal failure is kidney transplantation. Kidney transplantation involves surgically replacing a failed kidney with a healthy kidney from a living or deceased donor. This procedure offers the best long-term outcomes and the opportunity for a significantly improved quality of life for individuals with end-stage renal failure.

options.explanation: This is true at rest, but not during an action potential. During an action potential, the concentration of sodium ions outside the cell actually decreases slightly, as some sodium ions are drawn into the cell..

The correct answer is intracellular sodium is high and intracellular potassium is less. In a resting cell, the membrane potential is negative, meaning that there are more negatively charged ions inside the cell than outside. This is because the cell membrane is more permeable to potassium ions than sodium ions. Potassium ions are therefore more likely to diffuse out of the cell, while sodium ions are less likely to diffuse in. During an action potential, the cell membrane becomes more permeable to sodium ions. This causes sodium ions to rush into the cell, which depolarizes the membrane and creates an action potential. After the action potential, the cell membrane returns to its resting state. This is because the sodium channels that were open during the action potential close, and the potassium channels open. This allows potassium ions to diffuse out of the cell, which repolarizes the membrane. Therefore, in a cell that is experiencing an action potential, the intracellular sodium will be high and the intracellular potassium will be low.

questions.text: A pea plant has purple-colored flowers (dominant trait). To check the genotype, which of the following cross is performed
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Test cross with homozygous white-flowered plant
options.explanation: A test cross is performed to determine whether an organism showing a dominant trait (in this case, purple flowers) is homozygous dominant (PP) or heterozygous (Pp).To do this, the unknown plant is crossed with a homozygous recessive (pp) white-flowered plant.If all offspring have purple flowers, the unknown parent is homozygous dominant (PP).If half of the offspring have purple and half have white flowers, the unknown parent is heterozygous (Pp).Thus, this cross reveals the genotype of the dominant phenotype accurately.

options.text: Test cross with homozygous purple-flowered plant
options.explanation: Crossing with a homozygous dominant purple plant (PP) will not help identify the genotype of the unknown purple-flowered plant.Regardless of whether the unknown is PP or Pp, all offspring will have purple flowers, giving no clear distinction about the parent’s genotype.

options.text: Test cross with heterozygous purple-flowered plant
options.explanation: If the unknown purple-flowered plant is crossed with a heterozygous purple (Pp) plant, the offspring ratios would be unclear because both could contribute a dominant or recessive allele.This might result in a mix of purple and white offspring even if the unknown parent is homozygous, making it unreliable for determining genotype.

A hybrid cross (between two heterozygous plants, Pp × Pp) gives a 3:1 ratio of purple to white flowers.However, this is a monohybrid cross, not a test cross, and it doesn’t help determine whether the parent showing the dominant trait is PP or Pp.

questions.explanation: Option A: Test cross with homozygous white-flowered plant: This is the best option to determine the genotype of the plant with purple-colored flowers. If all of the offspring have purple flowers, then the plant with unknown genotype is homozygous dominant for purple flower color. If any of the offspring have white flowers, then the plant with an unknown genotype is heterozygous for purple flower color. Option B: Test cross with homozygous purple-flowered plant: This option would not be able to determine the genotype of the plant with purple-colored flowers. All of the offspring of the test cross would have purple flowers, regardless of the genotype of the plant with an unknown genotype. Option C: Test cross with heterozygous purple-flowered plant: This option would also not be able to determine the genotype of the plant with purple-colored flowers. There would be a 50:50 ratio of purple to white flowers in the offspring, regardless of the genotype of the plant with an unknown genotype. Option D: Hybrid cross: This option would not be able to determine the genotype of the plant with purple-colored flowers. There would be a 3:1 ratio of purple to white flowers in the offspring, regardless of the genotype of the plant with an unknown genotype.

questions.text: ΔH = ΔE + PΔV is the change in enthalpy at constant _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Volume: The change in enthalpy at constant volume is equal to the change in internal energy, ΔH = ΔE. This is because the pressure-volume work done by the system is zero at constant volume.

The equation ΔH = ΔE + PΔV is the change in enthalpy at constant pressure.Enthalpy (H) is a measure of the total energy of a system, including its internal energy (E) and the pressure-volume work done by the system. The change in enthalpy (ΔH) is equal to the change in internal energy (ΔE) plus the pressure-volume work done by the system (PΔV).The equation ΔH = ΔE + PΔV is only valid for changes in enthalpy that occur at constant pressure. If the pressure changes, then the equation must be modified to account for the work done by the system against the changing pressure.

Temperature: The change in enthalpy at constant temperature is equal to the change in heat, ΔH = q. This is because the heat is the only way that the system can transfer energy to its surroundings at constant temperature.

Mass: The change in enthalpy at constant mass is not a meaningful concept. This is because the mass of a system is not a thermodynamic variable. The thermodynamic variables that affect enthalpy are the pressure, the volume, and the temperature.

questions.text: Which of the following has the highest electrical conductivity?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aqueous sugar solutions generally do not conduct electricity well. While water itself can conduct electricity to some extent due to the presence of ions, sugar molecules do not dissociate into ions easily in solution. Therefore, the conductivity of an aqueous sugar solution is relatively low compared to other options.

Graphite is an allotrope of carbon and is known for its high electrical conductivity. The carbon atoms in graphite are arranged in a layered structure, forming sheets of hexagonal rings. These sheets have delocalized electrons that can move freely along the layers, allowing for efficient electron flow and high electrical conductivity. Therefore, solid graphite has a high electrical conductivity.

Solid sodium chloride, also known as table salt, is a crystalline structure composed of alternating sodium (Na+) and chloride (Cl-) ions. While ionic compounds like sodium chloride can conduct electricity when dissolved in water or molten state due to the movement of ions, they do not conduct electricity in the solid state. The fixed positions of the ions in the crystal lattice restrict the movement of charged particles, resulting in low electrical conductivity for solid sodium chloride.

Gaseous carbon dioxide consists of covalently bonded molecules. Since the molecules do not have a net charge and do not dissociate into ions in the gaseous state, gaseous carbon dioxide does not conduct electricity.

options.explanation: [Ar] 4s23d5: This option has the correct noble gas core, but it does not follow the Aufbau principle. The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy. The 4s orbital is lower in energy than the 3d orbital, so the electrons should fill the 4s orbital before they fill the 3d orbital.

[Ar] 4s1 3d5: This option follows the Aufbau principle. The noble gas core for Cr is [Ar], which means that the first 18 electrons should be filled up before the 3d and 4s orbitals are filled. Thus, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: The correct electronic configuration of Cr (Z = 24) is [Ar] 4s1 3d5. The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy. The 3d orbitals are lower in energy than the 4s orbitals, but the 3d orbitals are only half-filled in Cr, which makes them more stable than the 4s orbitals that are fully filled. Therefore, the electron configuration of Cr is [Ar] 4s1 3d5.

questions.text: The particle with highest e/m ratio

options.explanation: The particle with the highest e/m ratio is the electron. The e/m ratio is the charge-to-mass ratio of a particle. The electron has a charge of 1.602 × 10-19 coulombs and a mass of 9.109 × 10-31 kilograms. This gives the electron an e/m ratio of 1.7588 × 108 coulombs per kilogram. The high e/m ratio of the electron makes it a very important particle in physics. It is used in many different experiments, including the study of the structure of atoms and molecules. The electron is also responsible for many of the properties of matter, such as its electrical conductivity and its ability to absorb and emit light.
Proton: The proton has a charge of 1.602 × 10-19 coulombs and a mass of 1.6726 × 10-27 kilograms. This gives the proton an e/m ratio of 9.5784 × 107 coulombs per kilogram.
Neutron: The neutron has a charge of 0 coulombs and a mass of 1.6749 × 10-27 kilograms. This gives the neutron an e/m ratio of 0 coulombs per kilogram.

Meson: The meson is a group of particles that have a mass that is intermediate between the mass of an electron and the mass of a proton. The e/m ratio of a meson varies depending on the type of meson.

Neutron has zero charge. Proton has charge equal to electron but mass is much greater than electron. For proton, e/m ratio is 1/1 or 1. Since free radicals have unpaired electrons, they do not satisfy the octet rule for carbon, which typically requires four covalent bonds for carbon to achieve a complete octet in its valence shell. In other words, carbon in a free radical typically has fewer than eight electrons in its outer shell, making it highly reactive and seeking to form additional bonds to achieve stability.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is incorrect?

options.text: A solution freezes at a lower temperature than the pure solvent.
options.explanation: When a non-volatile solute is added to a solvent, the solution's vapor pressure decreases, causing it to freeze at a lower temperature. This is a correct statement regarding freezing point depression.

options.text: A solution boils at a higher temperature than the pure solvent.
options.explanation: The solution requires a higher temperature to reach the same vapor pressure as the pure solvent at its boiling point, resulting in boiling point elevation. This statement is correct.

options.text: 0.1 M NaCl solution and 0.1 M sugar solution have the same boiling point.
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. NaCl, being ionic, dissociates into two ions, resulting in more particles compared to sugar, which is non-ionic. Thus, NaCl solution has a higher boiling point elevation due to more solute particles.

options.text: Osmosis cannot take place without a semi-permeable membrane.
options.explanation: Osmosis requires a semi-permeable membrane to selectively allow solvent molecules to pass through, making this statement correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: 0.1 M NaCl solution and 0.1 M sugar solution have the same boiling point. This is incorrect because NaCl dissociates into two ions (Na+ and Cl-), effectively doubling the number of solute particles compared to sugar, which does not dissociate. This results in a higher boiling point elevation for the NaCl solution.Option A is correct as a solution indeed freezes at a lower temperature than the pure solvent due to the decrease in vapor pressure. Option B is also correct because the boiling point of a solution is elevated. Option D is correct since osmosis requires a semi-permeable membrane to occur.

options.explanation: lodoform Reaction: The iodoform test indicates the presence of an aldehyde or ketone in which one of the groups directly attached to the carbonyl carbon is a methyl group. Such a ketone is called a methyl ketone. In the iodoform test, the unknown is allowed to react with a mixture of excess iodine and excess hydroxide.lodoform test is done to detect the presence of the CH3CO group in organic compounds. When methyl ketone is heated with iodine in the presence of sodium hydroxide, yellow precipitate of iodoform is obtained. Hence methyl ketones are characterized iodoform test. In this reaction, methyl ketone is converted to the sodium salt of carboxylic acid.lodoform test is used to check the presence of carbonyl compounds with the structure R-CO–CH3The reaction of iodine, a base and a methyl ketone gives a yellow precipitate along with an "antiseptic" smell. It also tests positive for a few specific secondary alcohols that contain at least one methyl group in the alpha position.alpha position.Reaction Involved:R- CO-CH; +3I + 4NaOH → R- CO- ONa +CHI3 +3NaI +3H2O

Schiff's test is a chemical test used to check for the presence of aldehydes in a given analyte. This is done by reacting the analyte with a small quantity of a Schiffs reagent (which is the product formed in certain dye formulation reactions such as the reaction between sodium bisulfite and fuchsin). Ketones do not give this test.

Aliphatic aldehydes on treatment with Benedict's solution give a colored precipitate while aromatic aldehydes and ketones (except a-hydroxy methyl ketones) do not react with Benedict's solution. So methyl ketone also dont give this test.

Tollens' test, also known as silver-mirror test, is a qualitative laboratory test used to distinguish between an aldehyde and a ketone. It exploits the fact that aldehydes are readily oxidized whereas ketones are not.
Only aldehyde which lacks alpha hydrogen or do not have any alpha hydrogen to their carbonyl group undergo Cannizzaro reaction. Methyl ketone will not undergo this reaction.

questions.text: Which of the following fertilizers has the maximum percentage of nitrogen in solid state?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ammonia (NH3) has a molar mass of 17 g/mol, with nitrogen contributing 14 g/mol. The percentage of nitrogen in ammonia is calculated as:Percentage of N = (mass of N / total mass) x 100 = (14 / 17) x 100 ≈ 82.35%
Urea (NH2CONH2) has a molar mass of 60.06 g/mol. The nitrogen content is:Percentage of N = (2 x 14.006 / 60.06) x 100 ≈ 46.6%Thus, urea contains significantly less nitrogen than ammonia.

Diammonium hydrogen phosphate ((NH4)2HPO4) has a molar mass of 132 g/mol. The nitrogen content is calculated as:Percentage of N = (2 x 14 / 132) x 100 ≈ 10.6%This indicates that it contains much less nitrogen than ammonia.

Ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3) has a molar mass of 80.04 g/mol. The nitrogen content is:Percentage of N = (2 x 14 / 80.04) x 100 ≈ 34.9%While this is a significant nitrogen content, it is still lower than that of ammonia.

questions.explanation: Urea contains 46% nitrogen. the highest among common solid nitrogen fertilizers. Ammonium nitrate has about 34% N. and diammonium hydrogen phosphate has even less. Ammonia is not used as a solid fertilizer (it's typically gaseous or liquid), so urea gives the maximum solid-state nitrogen content.

questions.text: Acidic buffer can be prepared by mixing:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: For acidic buffer there must be strong base.

A solution whose pH is not altered to any great extent by the addition of small quantities of either an acid or base is called buffer solution. Buffer is also defined as the solution of reserve acidity or alkalinity which resists change of pH upon the addition of a small amount of acid or alkali. Many chemical reactions are carried out at a constant pH. In nature, there are many systems that use buffering for pH regulation. For example, the bicarbonate buffering system is used to regulate the pH of blood, and bicarbonate also acts as a buffer in the ocean.Types of buffer solution: (a) Acidic Buffer: It is formed by the mixture of weak acid and its salt with a strong base. Examples: (i) CH3COOH + CH3COONa, (i) HCN + NaCN, (i) Boric acid + Borax etc. (b) Basic Buffer: It is formed by the mixture of a weak base and its salt with strong acid. Examples: NH4OH + NH4CI, (ii) NH4OH + NH4NO3, (ii) Glycine + Glycine hydrochlorideAcid buffer has acidic pH and is prepared by mixing a weak acid and its salt with a strong base. An aqueous solution of an equal concentration of acetic acid and sodium acetate has a pH of 4.74.
This doesn't form any buffer.
It forms basic buffer.

questions.text: A very small value of 'Kc' for a reaction at equilibrium indicates:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: There will be large amount of reactants.

The equilibrium constant Kc indicates the ratio of the concentration of all substances involved in a chemical equilibrium reaction. It is used in particular in connection with the law of mass action. That is why it is often referred to as the mass action constant, more rarely as the equilibrium constant. In contrast to the position of the equilibrium, the value of the equilibrium constant depends only on the temperature and not on the concentration or the pressure. Kc= Concentration of products/ Concentration of reactants Specifically, for any reversible reaction at equilibrium aA + bB ⇄ cC + dD The equilibrium constant, Keq Or KC, is Kc = [C]c [D]d / [A]a [B]b The larger value of the equilibrium constant (>103) shows that forward reaction is favored i.e. the concentration of products is much larger than that of the reactants at equilibrium.For an example let's take an example of having products(numerator) greater than reactants(denominator) , obviously we will get positive and enough value of Kc , it means product are greater in concentration when Kc value is large. Intermediate value of equilibrium constant (10-3 to 103) show that the concentration of the reactants and products are comparable. Low value of equilibrium constant (<10-3) shows that backward reaction is favored i.e. concentration of reactants is much larger than that of products i.e. the reaction proceeds to a very small extent in the forward direction.

options.text: The rate of backward reaction is greater than that of the forward one
options.explanation: At equilibrium rate of forward and reverse reactions are equal.

options.text: The rate of forward reaction is greater than that of the backward one
options.explanation: At equilibrium rate of forward and reverse reactions are equal.

questions.explanation: If 'Kc' is a small number, it means that the equilibrium concentration of the reactants is large while that are products is small. In this case, the reaction, as written, will proceed to the left (resulting in an increase in the concentration of reactants).

options.explanation: Cathode rays are streams of electrons that are emitted from a cathode, which is a negatively charged electrode. Cathode rays are not similar to X-rays in nature because they are a form of particle radiation, while X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation.

Canal rays are streams of positive ions that are emitted from a cathode. Canal rays are not similar to X-rays in nature because they are a form of particle radiation, while X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation.

X-rays are similar in nature to gamma rays. Both X-rays and gamma rays are forms of electromagnetic radiation, and they have a very high frequency and a very short wavelength. They are also both ionising radiation, which means that they can damage DNA and other molecules in living cells. X-rays and gamma rays differ in their origin. X-rays are typically produced when high-energy electrons interact with matter, while gamma rays are emitted from the nuclei of radioactive atoms.

Beta rays are streams of electrons that are emitted from the nuclei of radioactive atoms. Beta rays are similar to X-rays in nature because they are both ionising radiation, which means that they can damage DNA and other molecules in living cells. However, beta rays are a form of particle radiation, while X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation.

questions.text: X rays are affected by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electric fields can only affect charged particles. Since X-rays are not charged, they will not be affected by electric fields.
Magnetic fields can only affect charged particles that are moving. Since X-rays are not charged, and they are also moving at the speed of light, they will not be affected by magnetic fields.

Electric fields can only affect charged particles. Since X-rays are not charged, they will not be affected by electric fields. Magnetic fields can only affect charged particles that are moving. Since X-rays are not charged, and they are also moving at the speed of light, they will not be affected by magnetic fields.

X-rays are not affected by electric or magnetic fields. This is because X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation, and electromagnetic radiation does not have a charge. Therefore, X-rays cannot be deflected by electric or magnetic fields. However, X-rays can be affected by other factors, such as the density of the material that they are passing through. The denser the material, the more likely it is that the X-rays will be absorbed. X-rays can also be scattered by electrons, which can reduce their penetrating power.

questions.text: The penetrating power of X-rays increases with:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The velocity of an X-ray is not directly related to its penetrating power. In fact, the velocity of an X-ray can actually decrease as its energy increases, because the higher-energy X-rays are more likely to interact with matter and be absorbed.

The penetrating power of X-rays increases with an increase in frequency. This is because the frequency of a wave is directly proportional to its energy, and the energy of an X-ray is what determines its ability to penetrate matter. Therefore, X-rays with a higher frequency will have a greater penetrating power than X-rays with a lower frequency.

The velocity of an X-ray is not directly related to its penetrating power. However, in general, the velocity of an X-ray will increase as its energy increases. Therefore, X-rays with a higher energy will also have a higher velocity.

The intensity of an X-ray is a measure of the number of X-rays per unit area. It does not have a direct effect on the penetrating power of X-rays. questions.text: Which of the following is not a S.I. base quantity:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Mass (kilogram, kg) is the amount of matter in an object. It is a fundamental property of matter that does not depend on the object's location or motion.
Intensity of light (candela, cd) is the amount of light emitted from a source in a particular direction. It is a measure of the brightness of light.

Velocity is a derived quantity, which means that it can be expressed in terms of the SI base quantities. For example, velocity is equal to the distance traveled divided by the time it takes to travel that distance, so it can be expressed in terms of the base quantities of length and time.
Length (meter, m) is the distance between two points. It is a fundamental property of space that does not depend on the location or motion of the points.
